Overlooking Jackson Square Park, 2 Horatio Street is one of the most coveted addresses in the West Village. Available furnished, with deeded storage. This landmarked building features a 24-hour doorman, live-in superintendent, laundry facilities, and pets, pied-à-terre, and co-purchasing permitted with board approval. Steps from world-class restaurants, shopping, and cultural destinations like the Whitney Museum and the High Line, this residence offers the best of New York City living. Don’t miss this rare offering in a historic Bing & Bing masterpiece.

笔记标题: 当我雇用某人时，我要寻找什么？
笔记文案: What do I look for when I hire someone?

In a neighborhood where new development is a rarity, La Maison Collette presents a unique opportunity to own a thoughtfully designed home by Paris Forino – where timeless design and quiet luxury come together.
How many hangers do you think can fit in closet number 2? Only in Palm Beach for $65M.

So this is on East 60th Street actually. We'll start here. Trader Joe's, Sixtieth and First. This used to be the Food Emporium before I guess that was bought or told whatever, the Trader Joe's. This is the grocery store right here under the 59th Street Bridge. That takes you into Queens where I went to buy tofu in the spring of 2008 and my debit card was declined. Which means there's no money in the account, uh, which was awful, and then I went to the 6 train and cried. Yeah, that feels like it was just yesterday. So then... July, a friend says, "Get your real estate license, you got to make money, you can't move home." So I get my real estate license over the summer. I start September 15th, 2008. The day Lehman Brothers files for bankruptcy. One-two punch. And then one of my first pitches ever—a guy that works in my office, his name was Ken. At that building right there, right across the street. Doon Alley? Right across the street on the 30th floor. It was 3.3 million. I didn't know the difference between a condo, a co-op, any of that. I just went in there and said, "I will do anything to get your home sold." I think it's the most valuable apartment I've ever seen in my whole life because it was the nicest apartment I've ever seen in my whole life at that point. Um, and he gave me a one-week listing to sell it. I did everything I could, I was grabbing people from the streets, all that, and I didn't sell it, and another broker sold it. And then, not that long ago, at the building right over there—that limestone gorgeous building right down there—a couple of blocks away from where I got my first ever listing for 3.3 million and didn't sell it... I sold that thing for about 12 million dollars per bedroom, for just under 40 million dollars, and I didn't even blink, because I just don't care anymore.

The largest sale of my entire career I did on Palm Beach Island three weeks ago over the phone. I can't do that in New York City or in Hong Kong, and I think there's a lot of reasons why that is happening. I don't think it's going to slow down anytime soon, but what I tell all of our agents, too, is on Palm Beach's worst day, it is still the greatest day for most people in our business around the world. So, just always remember.

笔记标题: 当我雇用某人时，我要寻找什么？
笔记文案: I get asked all the time, "How do I find great employees and great agents?" The first thing that I look for is energy. If you have bad energy or the wrong energy, and it's not going to fit in the ecosystem, that is a poison pill and your aquarium will turn green. Energy also includes people who are overly positive—"yes people" where nothing could ever go wrong. You don't want to surround yourself with those people, too. You want realness. Energy is first, then I look for empathy. You need to have employees, you need to have agents, who can empathize. You need employees who can lead meetings with empathy for how hard everyone's working or how tired everybody is. And then I look for enthusiasm. Be enthusiastic. If you aren't enthusiastic on your worst day, you'll never be enthusiastic on your best day. If you are an agent and you just sold a massive property, you see that check you worked so hard for, and you still don't have enthusiasm for what you're doing, you should quit. Get out while you're ahead. Always quit on your best day. Anyone can do that. And I try to surround myself with people who are better than me. You want to surround yourself with people who scare you just a little bit.

What makes a place a home? Is it the people, the parks? Is it the weather, the amenities? Maybe it's that restaurant, or the food truck, or that other restaurant near your neighborhood. Beyond your walls, home isn't just the place you go when the day's done, or four walls and a roof. It's the warmth of a community that knows your name. Home is where you live, where you love. Find your home here. Coming soon to 小红书。

Spotted something rare brewing in Greenwich Village. Only two new developments in eight years in this iconic neighborhood—that is practically unheard of. Here, new development is rare, and rarity always comes at a premium. The lucky few don't wait for listings; they buy the dream before it even hits the market. But now, something new is on the horizon, and trust me, everyone's going to want a piece.

笔记标题: 把时间留给需要好好努力的事！
笔记文案: There's no better way to keep working, and my goal is to stay so busy that I'm too busy to fail, right? So I'm doing too many things. I'm building a team. I'm constantly focused on winning the race that way. I'm like a horse with blinders on, you know? Like, I don't see everybody else or people's BS. I just want to do as much as I possibly can, and if I stop—like a horse, right?—then I'll fail. But if you just focus on that path ahead and you stay, stay like laser sharp, anything is possible. And don't worry about what everybody else is doing.

All right, breaking news, immediate release! Turns out reality TV didn't ruin my entire career, which is what everybody said it was going to do. This morning, The Wall Street Journal wrote an investigative article. Deep, deep, deep dive into S.H.I.P., which is our generative AI operating system for salespeople, where we replace administrative tasks for salespeople. We don't just make faster or better CRMs—we killed CRMs. You don't have to use one when you use S.H.I.P. You don't have to do buyer tours; it does it for you. How we did it, how we built it internally with our engineers, is in the article. Also, my feet, as you just saw, are in the article. Also, some sneak peeks into Owning Manhattan Season 2, which comes out December 5th, is in the article too. Huge thanks to The Wall Street Journal for writing a very transparent and honest look into what S.H.I.P. is and how it works. Um, really, really cool. What a wild morning this morning has been since this article came out. Anyway, go read it, go check it out. And to all of our engineers and staff and our agents at SERHANT. who made S.H.I.P. what it is...

笔记标题: 沟通秘诀：别只有不快时才发声
笔记文案: Conversations are only ever difficult if you treat them as a surprise. If you know the other side isn't expecting it, if you are rarely vocal with people about how you feel, you'll just keep how you feel inside until you're not happy, and you don't really ever have difficult conversations. Most managers assume, "Okay, I'm fine, how are you?" and you do your job, and they always forget to tell you when you're doing a good job. You need to have more daily conversations where we are expressing the good, the bad, and the ugly honestly.
